title: Benin 2025 Coup Attempt
content: Benin 2025
A small military faction calling itself the Comité Militaire pour la Refondation (CMR) failed to depose President Patrice Talon after a brief armed assault on the presidency.

Benin, once a bastion of democratic stability, faced a polarised political scene and authoritarian drift under President Talon. The move to create a second legislative chamber (Senate) and extend presidential terms stoked opposition fears of a permanent power grab. Simultaneously, the security environment in the north deteriorated significantly; 54 soldiers were killed in jihadist attacks in April 2025, leading to military grievances over negligence towards soldiers killed on the front and their families.

Early on December 7, gunfire erupted near the presidential residence in Cotonou. Eight-armed soldiers appeared on national television, led by Lieutenant-Colonel Pascal Tigri, claiming they had deposed the government to address the security failures against Al-Qaeda-linked groups. The attempt was quickly neutralized by loyalist forces with support from French intelligence and special forces. By the end of the day, the African Union and International IDEA issued strong condemnations, labeling the event a grave violation of constitutional order.

The failed putsch prompted a regional contagion alert, leading ECOWAS to consider military intervention to preserve civilian rule. President Talon utilized the aftermath to consolidate control, though international bodies urged him to respect term limits ahead of the 2026 elections. Analysts noted a possible AES influence, as Niger’s junta had reportedly been informed of the operation in advance.
references: Mesa, Aris. "Countering the Coup in Benin: A Jus ad Bellum Perspective." EJIL: Talk! (2025).
IRIS France. "Benin: What Does the 7 December Coup Attempt Reveal?" (2025).
African Union Commission. "Statement on the Attempted Military Coup in Benin." (Dec 7, 2025).
International IDEA. "Statement on the Attempted Coup in Benin." (Dec 8, 2025).
ACLED. "Two Weeks in Review: 15–23 December 2025."

title: Madagascar 2025 Coup Attempt
content: An elite unit known as CAPSAT overthrew President Andry Rajoelina following weeks of youth-led "Gen Z" protests over economic mismanagement and power shortages.

Madagascar suffered from extreme poverty, with over 75% of its 30 million people living below the poverty line. Public anger boiled over in September 2025 due to chronic electricity and water cuts. President Rajoelina’s attempts to bypass the legislature and dissolve the National Assembly to block an impeachment vote served as the final catalyst for the military’s intervention.

On October 12, the Army Personnel and Services Administration Corps (CAPSAT) seized the capital, Antananarivo, meeting little resistance as civilian police joined the demonstrators. The military unit’s leadership framed their action as answering the people’s calls. Rajoelina reportedly fled to Réunion island on a French military aircraft as the National Assembly proceeded to formally impeach him.

The African Union immediately suspended Madagascar’s membership, while the United Nations condemned the unconstitutional change of power. CAPSAT announced a two-year transitional period under military leadership. Domestically, the Malagasy ariary plummeted, and fuel imports were disrupted, plunging the nation into severe economic uncertainty.
references: Robert Lansing Institute. "October 2025 Coup in Madagascar: Causes and Consequences." 2025.
"2025 Malagasy coup d'état." Wikipedia/Consolidated Reports. 2025.
ReliefWeb. "SARI Global: Situation Report - Madagascar Political Crisis." 2025.
The Guardian. "‘Gen Z gave us the victory’: toppling Madagascar's leader." 2025.
Chin, John Joseph. "Coupvolution: The Malagasy Case." Academic Analysis. 2025.

title: Bolivia 2024 Coup Attempt
content: On June 26, 2024, a faction of the Bolivian Army led by General Juan José Zúñiga attempted to seize power by occupying the government headquarters in La Paz before retreating hours later due to a lack of institutional support.

Bolivia suffered from a severe economic crisis characterized by dwindling foreign reserves, fuel shortages, and high inflation. Simultaneously, the ruling Movement Towards Socialism (MAS) party split into two rival factions led by President Luis Arce and former President Evo Morales, who were on a collision course for the 2025 presidential candidacy.

The military uprising targeted President Arce’s democratically elected government. In the mid-afternoon of June 26, 2024, soldiers and armored vehicles led by General Zúñiga occupied Plaza Murillo, the site of the country’s main legislative and executive offices.

An armored vehicle rammed into the main gates of the Palacio Quemado, allowing troops to breach the building. Within the palace hallway, President Arce personally confronted Zúñiga, ordering the general to demobilize his forces and withdraw. Zúñiga claimed the military intended to "restore democracy" and release political prisoners like Jeanine Áñez. The situation reached a turning point when Arce appointed a new military high command, including General José Wilson Sánchez as the new army chief. Sánchez immediately ordered all mobilized personnel to return to their units. Lacking broader military backing and facing civilian mobilization in defense of democracy, the rebellious troops began their retreat from the plaza roughly three hours after the occupation began.

Authorities later arrested Zúñiga and former Navy commander Juan Arnez Salvador on charges of terrorism and armed uprising. The event intensified national polarization, with detractors alleging a self-coup (autogolpe) intended to bolster Arce’s falling popularity, while the administration maintained it was a direct assault on constitutional order.
references: BBC News Mundo. "Bolivia: cómo fue el intento de golpe de Estado que denunció el presidente Arce." June 26, 2024.
Inter-American Commission on Human Rights. "IACHR Condemns Coup Attempt in Bolivia." July 3, 2024.
CNN. "Bolivian General Arrested and Accused of Coup After Dramatic Showdown with President." June 27, 2024.
Mera Medina, María José. "The Aftermath of Bolivia's Failed Coup Attempt." October 9, 2024.
Scotland, Jennifer. "Divided Opinions After Failed Coup Underscore Bolivia's Political Fragility." RUSI, August 6, 2024.

title: Gabon 2023 Coup Attempt
content: On August 30, 2023, senior military officers in Gabon seized power, placing President Ali Bongo Ondimba under house arrest minutes after he declared victory in a disputed general election.

The coup ended the 56-year dynastic rule of the Bongo family, which was plagued by allegations of systemic corruption, election fraud, and economic inequality. Despite Gabon's status as an upper middle-income oil producer, approximately one-third of the population lived in poverty with high youth unemployment.

The primary target of the military intervention was President Ali Bongo Ondimba. Following the electoral authority's announcement of a victory for Bongo with 64.27% of the vote—results many observers deemed implausible—the military moved to annul the process. A group of high-ranking personnel, identifying as the Committee for the Transition and Restoration of Institutions (CTRI), appeared on national television to declare they were seizing power to defend peace and end the current regime. The decisive force was the Republican Guard, led by General Brice Oligui Nguema, Bongo’s cousin and former security chief.

The coup was largely nonviolent and received broad public support, with citizens celebrating in the streets of Libreville and Port-Gentil. Military units dissolved all state institutions, including the government, Senate, National Assembly, and Constitutional Court, and briefly closed the country's borders to consolidate control.

General Nguema was named transitional president, promising a return to civilian rule by 2025 while simultaneously consolidating power through a new constitution. Reports from 2025, however, note a landslide victory for Nguema, which some classify as a repackaging of military rule. Ali Bongo was released from house arrest into retirement, though his family faced detention for high treason. The country remains suspended from the African Union.
references: Beaumont, Peter. "Gabon Military Officers Declare Coup After Ali Bongo Wins Disputed Election." The Guardian, August 30, 2023.
Gaulme, François, and Paul Melly. "Gabon: Has an Almost-Exemplary Transition Produced a New Political Model?" Ifri Editorials, October 1, 2025.
Hoinathy, Remadji, and Nirvaly Mooloo. "Gabon and the Role of Political Transitions in Preventing Coups." ISS Today, October 25, 2023.
Khandelwal, Nandini. "One Year On: Gabon's Coup and Its Impact on Governance." Indian Council of World Affairs, October 25, 2024.
McGoldrick, Maura. "Gabon 2025: Civilian Future or Military Rule Repackaged?" Inclusive Peace, 2024.
Nithyashree, R. B. "Gabon: Coup Amid a Political Crisis and Contentious Election." NIAS-IPRI-KAS Conflict Weekly, August 31, 2023.
U.S. Department of State. "2023 Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Gabon." 2024.

title: Niger 2023 Coup Attempt
content: On July 26, 2023, members of Niger’s presidential guard led by General Abdourahamane Tchiani detained President Mohamed Bazoum in the capital and seized executive power, citing deteriorating national security and governance.

Niger had recently achieved its first peaceful, democratic transfer of power in 2021; however, the country faced chronic challenges including systemic poverty, corruption, and a surge in jihadist violence across the Sahel. These grievances, combined with rumored internal power struggles between Bazoum and military leadership, provided a pretext for the intervention.

The primary target of the coup was President Mohamed Bazoum, who was blockaded and detained within his palace in Niamey by his own Presidential Guard. Initially, the uprising appeared to be a localized mutiny, and the broader military, the Nigerien Armed Forces (FAN), signaled readiness to defend the constitutional order. However, the situation reached a turning point when the military high command, led by General Abdou Sidikou Issa, declared their support for the mutineers to avoid a deadly confrontation between units. On state television, Colonel-Major Amadou Abdramane announced the formation of the National Council for the Safeguard of the Homeland (CNSP). This announcement confirmed the dissolution of the constitution and the suspension of all government agencies. Key individuals involved in the consolidation of power included General Tchiani and General Moussa Salaou Barmou, a U.S.-trained special forces commander. Civilian protests in support of Bazoum were dispersed by gunfire from the Presidential Guard before the junta established full territorial control.

The coup resulted in the indefinite detention of President Bazoum and the imposition of severe ECOWAS regional sanctions that devastated Niger’s economy. Politically, the junta shifted away from Western security partnerships, expelling French and U.S. troops in favor of military cooperation with Russia and fellow juntas in Mali and Burkina Faso.
references: Ajala, Olayinka. "What Caused the Coup in Niger? An Expert Outlines Three Driving Factors." The Conversation, July 31, 2023.
Al Jazeera. "Niger military coup: What you need to know." July 27, 2023.
European Parliament. "The Coup in Niger." Briefing, September 2023.
McCullough, Aoife, and Adam Sandor. "How a mutiny became a coup: The politics of counterinsurgency and international military partnerships in Niger." African Affairs, 2023.
Turse, Nick. "Niger Coup Leader Joins Long Line of U.S.-Trained Mutineers." The Intercept, July 27, 2023.

title: Sudan 2023 Coup Attempt
content: The 2023 conflict in Sudan began as a violent struggle for national control between two rival generals whose former alliance collapsed into a catastrophic civil war.

Following the 2019 ousting of dictator Omar al-Bashir, a fragile power-sharing agreement between military and civilian leaders began to unravel. A military coup in October 2021 further stymied democratic progress. Tensions then escalated over the timeline for integrating paramilitary forces into the national army.

On April 15, 2023, fierce fighting erupted in Khartoum and quickly spread across the country. The conflict pitted the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF), led by General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, against the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), commanded by Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo, also known as Hemedti. This was not a typical coup against a civilian government but a struggle within the security sector itself, as the RSF sought to seize strategic sites including the airport and Presidential Palace. The target of the RSF’s initial offensive was the SAF's military command and the de facto leadership of al-Burhan. Neither side achieved a quick victory, as the SAF responded with airstrikes while the RSF leveraged its urban mobility. The failure to reach a rapid outcome transitioned the event from a power grab into a protracted civil war.

The war has created the world's largest displacement crisis, with over 13 million people forced from their homes. It has decimated the economy and caused widespread famine. Politically, the country remains fractured, with the SAF governing from Port Sudan while the RSF controls significant territory in the west and south.
references: Council on Foreign Relations. "Civil War in Sudan." Global Conflict Tracker. Updated December 16, 2025.
Kiros, Kidane. "The Ongoing War in Sudan and Its Implications for the Security and Stability of the Horn of Africa and Beyond." Policy Center for the New South, October 2024.
Krüger, Steffen, Gregory Meyer, and Nils Wörmer. "From Spring to Tragedy: The Unnoticed War in Sudan." Konrad-Adenauer-Stiftung, 2024.
Kurtz, Gerrit. "Power Relations in Sudan after the Fall of Bashir: From Revolution to War." Stiftung Wissenschaft und Politik, May 2024.
Lindén, Karolina. "Civil War in Sudan: A Struggle for Political Power and Economic Interests." Swedish Defence Research Agency, December 2024.
Mbaku, John Mukum. "Ending Sudan's Civil War: A Roadmap to Peace and Reconciliation." Brookings Institution, January 20, 2026.

title: Sao Tome and Principe 2022 Coup Attempt
sourceLinkLabel: 2022 Sao Tome and Principe Coup Attempt
content: The 2022 coup attempt in Sao Tome and Principe was a sudden overnight raid on military barracks that shattered the peace of one of Africa’s most stable democracies.

Sao Tome and Principe is a multiparty democracy with a history of five failed coup attempts since 1991. In September 2022, the Independent Democratic Action party won legislative elections, leading to a peaceful transfer of power. Prime Minister Patrice Trovoada was sworn in just weeks before the attack.

Shortly after midnight on November 25, 2022, a small group of gunmen launched an attack on the national army headquarters in the capital city. The target of this coup attempt was the newly established government of Prime Minister Patrice Trovoada. Four civilians entered the barracks in search of weapons and abducted a sentry as a hostage. The group was led by Arlécio Costa, a former mercenary who had previously attempted a coup in 2003. Costa was reportedly disgruntled over the seizure of land by the previous administration. The military responded quickly, engaging in a battle that lasted until approximately 6:00 a.m. During the fighting, the army captured three of the intruders at the base. Authorities also arrested Delfim Neves, the former president of the National Assembly, on suspicion of supporting the plot. By dawn, the military had successfully thwarted the attempt and neutralized the attackers.

The event ended in tragedy when four suspects, including Arlécio Costa, died from torture while in military custody. While the government remains in power, the incident damaged the nation's democratic reputation. Several soldiers faced charges for the custodial deaths, and the army chief subsequently resigned.
references: Associated Press. "Prime Minister: Coup Thwarted in Sao Tome and Principe." November 25, 2022.
European Union External Action Service. "Sao Tome and Principe: Statement on the Coup Attempt." November 29, 2022.
Farmhouse, Waldemar. "Examining the Sao Tome and Principe Coup Attempt." Parley Policy Initiative, March 3, 2024.
Freedom House. "Sao Tome and Principe: Freedom in the World 2024 Country Report." 2024.
Reuters. "Sao Tome and Principe Government Thwarts Overnight Coup Attempt." November 25, 2022.
U.S. Department of State. "2022 Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Sao Tome and Principe." 2022.

title: Burkina Faso 2022 Coup Attempt
content: On September 30, 2022, Captain Ibrahim Traoré, a 34-year-old artillery officer frustrated with military leadership, led a coup that ousted Lieutenant Colonel Paul-Henri Sandaogo Damiba, the junta leader who had himself seized power in January 2022, consolidating military control over the transitional government.

Burkina Faso's political crisis intensified following the January 23–24, 2022, military coup that ousted President Roch Marc Christian Kaboré. Damiba, promising to restore security against a worsening Islamist insurgency, was initially welcomed by Burkinabe society frustrated by the civilian government's inability to contain jihadist violence. However, by September 2022, the security situation had deteriorated dramatically. Islamist groups controlled nearly 40 percent of Burkina Faso's territory; violence had surged, and military officers and frontline troops under Damiba's command complained that he prioritized his own political interests over defeating insurgents.

On September 30, Traoré and disaffected military officers arrested Damiba and announced his deposition via state television, citing his failure to address the country's deteriorating security situation. They suspended the constitution, dissolved the transitional government and legislature, and imposed a nationwide curfew from 9 pm to 5 am. Traoré declared himself head of state and head of the Patriotic Movement for Safeguard and Restoration. Initially, Damiba attempted to flee toward the French military base at Camp Kamboinsin to potentially mount a counter-coup; however, this claim is disputed by Traoré. On October 2, religious and community leaders mediated Damiba's formal resignation, and he fled to exile in Togo after securing guarantees including protection for his allies and respect for the ECOWAS transition timeline.

The international community condemned the coup immediately. ECOWAS, the African Union, the United Nations, France, and the United States all protested the unconstitutional seizure of power. However, Traoré promised to honor Damiba's ECOWAS commitment to restore civilian rule by July 2024. The coup set Burkina Faso on a trajectory of deepening military autocracy, expanding human rights abuses, and increasing isolation from international partners, reversing what remained of the country's democratic transition.
references: Africa Center for Strategic Studies. "Understanding Burkina Faso's Latest Coup." September 30, 2022.
Al Jazeera. "Ibrahim Traore Declared Burkina Faso President After Coup." October 6, 2022.
Human Rights Watch. "Burkina Faso: Coup Raises Serious Rights Concerns." September 30, 2022.
ISS Africa. "Burkina Faso: Progress and Problems After Two Years of Transition." February 21, 2024.
Ofori-Mensah, Michael, and Gavin Harvey. "Burkina Faso at a Crossroads Against Human Suffering and Instability." Transparency International Defence & Security, November 7, 2024.

title: Burkina Faso 2022 Coup Attempt
content: On January 24, 2022, Lieutenant Colonel Paul-Henri Sandaogo Damiba led a military coup that ousted democratically elected President Roch Marc Christian Kaboré, citing the government's failure to contain a worsening Islamic insurgency.

Burkina Faso's transition to democracy following the 2014 popular uprising that ended Blaise Compaoré's 27-year rule proved fragile. Though President Kaboré won peaceful elections in 2015 and again in 2020, rising jihadist violence that began in 2015 and killed thousands destabilized the country. By late 2021, widespread public dissatisfaction mounted as the military and civilian population suffered repeated defeats against al-Qaeda and Islamic State-affiliated groups, with the November 2021 Inata attack killing 49 soldiers. Kaboré's December 2021 military reshuffle and the prime minister's resignation came too late to restore confidence.

On January 23–24, gunfire erupted at the presidential residence and military barracks in Ouagadougou. Soldiers from multiple garrisons participated, coordinating attacks that overwhelmed state security. Military units detained President Kaboré at the Sangoulé Lamizana barracks. By the afternoon of January 24, Captain Sidsore Kader Ouedraogo announced on state television that the Patriotic Movement for Safeguard and Restoration (MPSR), led by Lieutenant Colonel Paul-Henri Damiba, had seized power. The 41-year-old Damiba, recently promoted to command the capital's third military region, had military training in Paris and authored a book on West African counterterrorism. The coup dissolved parliament, suspended the constitution, and imposed a nationwide curfew. In military custody, Kaboré signed a resignation letter, and soldiers declared a promised return to civilian rule within a reasonable time frame.

Damiba initially received public support and limited international engagement, though ECOWAS and the African Union condemned the coup and demanded constitutional restoration. However, Damiba's inability to improve security—violence increased 23 percent during his eight months in office—led to his own overthrow by Captain Ibrahim Traoré on September 30, 2022. The January coup thus marked Burkina Faso's continued instability and military dominance over democratic governance.
references: Chin, John, Haleigh Bartos, and Aleksaundra Handrinos. "What Burkina Faso’s Tragic History Teaches Us." Journal of Democracy, October 2024.
Heo, Sora. "Vicious Cycles: Burkina Faso’s Coup Quandary." International Relations Review, January 3, 2023.
Human Rights Watch. "Burkina Faso: Coup Puts Rights at Risk." January 27, 2022.
United Nations. "Secretary-General Following Events in Burkina Faso with Deep Concern." January 24, 2022.

title: Sudan 2021 Coup Attempt
content: On October 25, 2021, General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, chairman of Sudan's Sovereignty Council, orchestrated a military coup that dissolved the transitional civilian-led government and detained Prime Minister Abdalla Hamdok, reversing Sudan's democratic transition just months before the military was scheduled to transfer power to civilian leadership.

Sudan's August 2019 power-sharing arrangement between military and civilian leaders proved unsustainable. Under a 39-month transitional framework, the military would govern the first 21 months before transferring leadership of the Sovereignty Council to civilian representatives in November 2021. However, al-Burhan and military officers resisted civilian-led security sector reform, international pressure to refer ousted dictator Omar al-Bashir to the International Criminal Court, and investigations into military responsibility for the 2019 Khartoum massacre. Economic deterioration, inflation reaching approximately 200 percent, unemployment, and food insecurity created widespread public discontent that Prime Minister Hamdok's civilian government could not adequately address. The failed September 21 coup attempt by al-Bashir loyalists provided a pretext for military intervention.

On October 25, military forces arrested Hamdok and cabinet members, dissolved the Sovereignty Council and transitional government, suspended constitutional provisions granting civilians power, and declared a state of emergency. Al-Burhan justified the coup as necessary to prevent civil war and preserve national unity. Hamdok, initially held at al-Burhan's residence, was later placed under house arrest. Hundreds of thousands of pro-democracy protesters took to the streets of Khartoum, and security forces responded with violence, killing at least eight people and injuring over 140 demonstrators.

The African Union suspended Sudan's membership, while ECOWAS, the United Nations, the United States, and the European Union condemned the coup and withheld aid. On November 21, 2021, after international mediation, Hamdok and al-Burhan signed a power-sharing agreement that temporarily reinstated Hamdok as prime minister. However, his resignation in January 2022 ultimately consolidated military rule.
references: Arab Center Washington DC. "Sudan Coup: The Regional Interference behind a Faltering Transition." December 11, 2021.
Middle East Policy Council. "Sudanese Government Instability and Military Coup." March 20, 2024.
CNN. "Sudan coup explained: The military has taken over in Sudan." October 26, 2021.
Middle East Eye. "Sudan coup 2021: Who is Abdel Fattah al-Burhan?" October 26, 2021.
Human Rights Watch. "Sudan: Military Takeover Threatens Rights." October 26, 2021.

title: Sudan 2021 Coup Attempt
content: On September 21, 2021, al-Bashir loyalists and military officers from Sudan's armored divisions staged a failed coup attempt against the Sovereignty Council led by General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, seeking to reverse Sudan's 2019 democratic transition and prevent their former leader's extradition to the International Criminal Court.

Sudan's political transition following the 2019 overthrow of long-time dictator Omar al-Bashir proved deeply fragile. The August 2019 power-sharing arrangement established a Sovereignty Council composed of military and civilian members, with al-Burhan as chair, while economist Abdalla Hamdok led a civilian transitional government. However, the military resisted civilian-led security sector reform, ICC referrals of al-Bashir, and investigations into the 2019 Khartoum massacre that implicated officers in serious abuses. Economic deterioration, inflation, and government ineffectiveness further strained the tenuous civil-military partnership.

On September 21, dawn attacks began when Major General Abdel-Baqi Bakrawi led officers and soldiers from the armored divisions in an attempt to seize the Sovereignty Council, military headquarters, and state television in Omdurman. Loyal military forces repelled the plotters, who eventually surrendered inside a weapons depot. By the end of the day, more than 40 military and civilian supporters of al-Bashir had been arrested. Prime Minister Hamdok condemned the orchestrated coup attempt as an extension of al-Bashir's legacy of undermining democratic transition, while the United Nations warned the event could derail Sudan's political progress.

The failed September 21 coup has been described by analysts as a trial balloon, revealing vulnerabilities in the transition that General al-Burhan would exploit just 34 days later during his successful October 25, 2021 coup, which dissolved the civilian government and reversed many of the democratic gains achieved after 2019.
references: Arab Center Washington DC. "Sudan Coup: The Regional Interference behind a Faltering Transition." December 11, 2021.
Voice of America. "Sudan Blames Failed Coup Attempt on Bashir Loyalists." September 21, 2021.
Al Jazeera. "Sudan's PM says coup plotters were military and non-military." September 21, 2021.
Middle East Policy Council. "Sudanese Government Instability and Military Coup." March 20, 2024.
"September 2021 Sudanese coup attempt." Wikipedia.

title: Guinea 2021 Coup Attempt
content: Military special forces seized power in Guinea on September 5, 2021, arresting President Alpha Condé and dissolving the nation's democratic institutions.

Alpha Condé became Guinea's first democratically elected president in 2010 but grew increasingly authoritarian. In 2020, he oversaw a controversial constitutional amendment to bypass term limits. His third-term re-election was marred by violent state repression, widespread poverty, and allegations of government corruption.

On the morning of September 5, 2021, an elite military unit known as the Special Forces Group launched an assault in the capital city of Conakry. Led by Colonel Mamadi Doumbouya, a former French legionnaire, the unit targeted the Presidential Palace in the Kaloum neighborhood. Heavy gunfire broke out as the special forces moved to arrest President Alpha Condé. During the brief but intense combat, between eight and twenty military personnel were killed. The special forces successfully detained Condé, later circulating images of the barefoot and disheveled leader in their custody. Doumbouya appeared on state television draped in a national flag to announce the takeover, stating the military had seized power to end political personalization and address economic mismanagement.

Colonel Doumbouya was sworn in as interim president and established the National Committee for Reunification and Development to oversee the government. President Condé remained under house arrest without charge. International bodies including ECOWAS and the African Union suspended Guinea's membership and demanded a return to civilian rule.
references: Akuamoah, Ernest Mensah. "The Coup in Guinea: Causes and Consequences." Australian Institute of International Affairs, December 28, 2021.
BBC News. "Guinea Coup: Who Is Col Mamady Doumbouya?" October 1, 2021.
Bemgba, Paul Terlumun, and Ene Vincent-Orugbo. "From Democracy to Dictatorship: The Economic Impact of Guinea's 2021 Coup." World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews, 2024.
Freedom House. "Guinea: Freedom in the World 2022 Country Report." 2022.
U.S. Department of State. "2021 Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Guinea." 2022.
Zamfir, Ionel. "Political Crisis in Guinea." European Parliamentary Research Service, September 2021.

title: Mali 2021 Coup Attempt
content: On May 24, 2021, Colonel Assimi Goïta, Mali's vice president and leader of a military coup nine months prior, orchestrated a second coup by arresting interim President Bah Ndaw and Prime Minister Moctar Ouane, effectively consolidating military control over the transitional government.

Mali's political crisis intensified following the August 18, 2020 military coup that ousted President Ibrahim Boubacar Keïta. International pressure from ECOWAS and the African Union forced the junta to establish a civilian-led transitional government, with Ndaw inaugurated as interim president on September 25, 2020, and Goïta as vice president. However, this civilian arrangement masked the military's continued dominance over state affairs.

On May 24, 2021, the interim government announced a cabinet reshuffle that removed two coup leaders—Colonel Sadio Camara (Defense Minister) and Colonel Modibo Kone (Security Minister)—from ministerial positions without consulting Goïta. That evening, military forces arrested both Ndaw and Ouane and detained them at the Soundiata Keïta military camp in Kati. On May 25, Goïta announced the coup on national television, claiming the leaders had violated the transition charter and attempted to sabotage the process. Both leaders resigned under detention on May 26 and were released on May 27. Mali's Constitutional Court subsequently declared Goïta interim president, and he was sworn in on June 7. Choguel Kokalla Maïga, from the M5-RFP opposition coalition, was appointed prime minister in June.

The international community condemned the coup immediately. ECOWAS suspended Mali and imposed sanctions on transitional leaders, while the African Union, United Nations, France, and the United States demanded a return to democratic governance. Mali's election timeline collapsed; instead of elections in February 2022, the junta extended the transition to 2026, effectively entrenching military rule.
references: Akinola, Adeoye O., and Ratidzo Makombe. "Rethinking the Resurgence of Military Coups in Africa." SAGE Open, 2025.
Cold-Ravnkilde, Signe Marie, and Almamy Sylla. "The rise of Mali's videomen as cybercombatants." International Affairs, 2024.
Al Jazeera. "Mali's former coup leader takes power after president's arrest." May 25, 2021.
Human Rights Watch. "Mali." World Report 2022.
Mahmoud, Sohaib, and Mohamed Taifouri. "The Coups d'État of the Sahel Region." Arab Center Washington DC, September 27, 2023.

title: Chad 2021 Coup Attempt
content: The sudden battlefield death of President Idriss Déby in April 2021 triggered an immediate military takeover in Chad, where a junta led by his son bypassed constitutional succession to seize control.

Idriss Déby had ruled Chad for three decades after seizing power in 1990. His tenure was marked by autocratic control, frequent rebel incursions, and a 2021 election widely viewed as fraudulent. Despite domestic repression, he remained a key Western ally in regional counterterrorism efforts.

On April 20, 2021, the Chadian army announced that President Idriss Déby had died from injuries sustained while visiting front lines during fighting against FACT rebels from Libya. Under the constitution, power should have transferred to the President of the National Assembly. Instead, a group of fifteen army generals dissolved the government and parliament and formed a Transitional Military Council (CMT). This junta installed the president’s son, General Mahamat Idriss Déby, as the new leader. The military immediately suspended the constitution, closed borders, and imposed a nationwide curfew to consolidate its authority. International allies such as France endorsed the new leadership despite its unconstitutional nature, citing the need for regional stability.

The takeover led to a prolonged transitional period initially set for eighteen months and later extended. The CMT faced domestic protests that were met with violent repression and arrests. Politically, the event ensured the Déby family maintained its grip on power despite promises of a return to civilian rule.
references: Harding, Andrew. "Chad's President Idriss Déby Dies After Clashes with Rebels." BBC News, April 20, 2021.
International Crisis Group. "Getting Chad's Transition on Track." September 30, 2021.
Institute for Security Studies. "Inconsistencies Cost the AU in Chad." June 20, 2023.
Rosenhart, Eline. "The Death of Chad's Warrior President." Ifriqiya, May 11, 2021.
Tubiana, Jérôme. "Le Tchad sous et après Déby." Politique africaine, 2021.
U.S. Department of State. "2021 Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Chad." 2022.

title: Myanmar 2021 Coup Attempt
content: On February 1, 2021, Myanmar's military, known as the Tatmadaw, staged a coup that detained State Counsellor Aung San Suu Kyi, President Win Myint, and numerous other civilian leaders, ending the country's fragile democratic transition and returning Myanmar to military rule.

Myanmar's tentative democracy emerged after decades of military rule, with elections in 2015 bringing Aung San Suu Kyi's National League for Democracy (NLD) to power. Although constitutional restrictions prevented Suu Kyi from serving as president, she held significant authority as State Counsellor. However, the 2008 military-drafted constitution preserved military influence by reserving parliamentary seats and granting veto power over amendments. Tensions escalated after the November 2020 elections delivered a landslide victory to the NLD, while the military-aligned Union Solidarity and Development Party (USDP) performed poorly. The military alleged widespread electoral fraud without credible evidence.

In early morning raids on February 1, military forces arrested Suu Kyi, Win Myint, NLD lawmakers, cabinet members, and civil society activists across the country. The military shut down internet and communications and declared a one-year state of emergency under constitutional provisions. Vice President Myint Swe, a military appointee, became acting president and transferred authority to Commander-in-Chief Min Aung Hlaing. The military promised new elections, though analysts widely viewed the coup as preemptive and unconstitutional.

International condemnation was swift and widespread. Governments including the United States, United Kingdom, France, Australia, and the European Union denounced the coup. Myanmar’s ambassador to the United Nations, Kyaw Moe Tun, publicly opposed the military before being dismissed. Massive pro-democracy protests erupted nationwide, and security forces responded with lethal force, leading to a prolonged period of repression and instability that continues today.
references: "2021 Myanmar coup d'état." Wikipedia.
Council on Foreign Relations. "Myanmar's Troubled History: Coups, Military Rule, and Ethnic Conflict." January 31, 2022.
Human Rights Watch. "Myanmar: Military Coup Kills Fragile Democracy." February 1, 2021.
CNN. "Myanmar coup explained: Why Aung San Suu Kyi was detained by the military." March 1, 2021.
Al Jazeera. "Myanmar's military stages coup d'etat: Live news." February 2021.
